name: Kodagu Devara Kaadu
biodiversityInfo: Home to over 200 species of plants, 85 bird species, and rare endemic orchids. The dense canopy hosts Malabar giant squirrel and lion-tailed macaque.
scientificInfo: Primary tropical evergreen forest fragment. Acts as seed bank for regional flora. Houses 14 endemic Western Ghats tree species.
climateRole: Regulates micro-climate within 5km radius. Reduces ambient temperature by 3-4°C. Sequesters approximately 450 tonnes of carbon annually.
waterRole: Primary recharge zone for Cauvery river tributaries. Maintains 3 perennial streams and 6 seasonal springs.
legendStory: According to Kodava oral tradition, Igguthappa, the supreme deity of the Kodavas, first descended to Earth in this grove. The ancient Peepal tree at its center is believed to be 400 years old and is worshipped as the deity's throne. Families visit every Kail Podh festival to offer prayers and first harvest.
traditions: Annual Kail Podh festival with first harvest offerings. No axes or tools permitted inside. Women lead the procession during Puthari.
ritualPractices: Offering of rice beer (kachampuli), fresh coconuts, and flower garlands. Community elder recites ancestral invocations before entry.
waterSources: Three perennial streams feeding into Cauvery tributaries. Ancient stone-lined well inside grove boundary.
ecologicalImportance: Critical corridor for Western Ghats wildlife. Prevents soil erosion on steep Coorg slopes. Hosts mycorrhizal networks vital for forest regeneration.
rareMedicinalPlants: Kaadu Soppina Soppu (wild spinach), Nela Bevu (bitter bark), Atti (cluster fig), Aadusoge (medicinal creeper)

name: Sirsi Bana
biodiversityInfo: Dense semi-evergreen patch with 160+ plant species. Rich in epiphytes, ferns, and rare medicinal climbers. Habitat for Malabar pit viper.
scientificInfo: One of the few remaining lowland Bana patches in Uttara Kannada. Contains 8 IUCN Red Listed plant species. Critical seed dispersal hub.
climateRole: Maintains humidity corridor between Western Ghats and coastal Karnataka. Local fog harvesting from canopy provides 200+ litres daily in dry season.
waterRole: Feeds the Aghanashini river with groundwater through deep-root systems. Community borewells in 2km radius draw from this recharge zone.
legendStory: Marikamba, the fierce protector goddess, is said to have turned a greedy merchant to stone when he attempted to cut trees for profit. The stone formation can still be seen at the grove entrance. Every two years, the Sirsi Marikamba Jatra attracts 500,000 devotees who vow to protect this sacred space.
traditions: Marikamba Jatra biennial festival. Sacred thread tied around boundary trees by devotees. No non-vegetarian food within 1km radius.
ritualPractices: Pradakshina (circumambulation) of the main deity tree. Offering of turmeric, vermilion, and coconut. Community vow renewal ceremony.
waterSources: Two seasonal streams. Ancient step-well (pushkarini) at grove center used for ritual bathing.
ecologicalImportance: Pollinators hub for surrounding cardamom and pepper plantations. Controls invasive species spread. Root networks prevent landslides on slopes.

name: Nagabana of Sullia
biodiversityInfo: Concentrated serpent habitat grove. 45 plant species with high density. King cobras and Indian rock pythons breed here undisturbed. Rich soil biology.
scientificInfo: Nagabanas function as micro-reserves for herpetofauna. pH-balanced leaf litter creates ideal king cobra nesting conditions. Zero pesticide zone.
climateRole: Urban heat island buffer for Sullia town. Maintains 2°C cooler microclimate. Intercepts monsoon runoff reducing flash flood risk.
waterRole: Sacred pond (Naga Kere) within grove recharges shallow aquifers. Used for ritual but maintains natural ecology.
legendStory: It is believed that Vasuki, the cosmic serpent and companion of Lord Shiva, manifested here after a great drought. He commanded villagers to protect the grove in exchange for rain. Since that covenant, the grove has never been touched by axe. Any family violating this oath reportedly suffers misfortune.
traditions: Naga Panchami festival with milk offering to serpents. No footwear permitted. Children brought here for Nama Karana (naming ceremony).
ritualPractices: Pouring of turmeric water and milk around the sacred anthills. Clay serpent idols placed at grove boundary. Silent entry required.
waterSources: Sacred Naga Kere (serpent pond). Natural spring emerging from granite outcrop.
ecologicalImportance: Primary breeding site for king cobra in Dakshina Kannada. Anthill ecosystem supports mongoose, monitor lizard population. Rodent control for farms.
rareMedicinalPlants: Visha Nirmoolini (anti-venom herb), Tagara (valerian), Brahmi, Punarnava

name: Kudremukh Kans
biodiversityInfo: The largest Kans forest in Chikkamagaluru. 320 plant species including 22 endemic to Western Ghats. Elephant corridor. Leopard territory.
scientificInfo: Shola-grassland ecosystem interface. Acts as buffer for Kudremukh National Park. Critical for maintaining Bhadra river flow. Bryophyte and lichen richness.
climateRole: Cloud forest capturing horizontal precipitation. Provides 30% of Bhadra river's non-monsoon flow. Prevents desertification of plateau.
waterRole: Origin of Bhadra river. Provides drinking water to 3 million people downstream. Dense root system holds 10x more water than degraded land.
legendStory: Bhadra Devi, goddess of the Bhadra river, is said to reside in the ancient moss-covered stones of this Kans. Fishermen and farmers believe she controls monsoon intensity. During droughts, the community performs the Bhadra Puja ceremony, and historically rains have followed within 3 days.
traditions: Bhadra Puja during drought. Annual community patrol of boundaries. Tree planting during Vana Mahotsava by school children.
ritualPractices: Immersion of clay Bhadra Devi idol in Bhadra river. Recitation of river-origin hymns. Collective vow of protection by all households.
waterSources: Source of Bhadra river. 12 perennial springs. High-altitude peat bogs retaining monsoon water.
ecologicalImportance: Elephant movement corridor between Kudremukh and Bhadra Wildlife Sanctuary. Critically endangered lion-tailed macaque habitat. Carbon sink of national importance.
rareMedicinalPlants: Kutki, Chirayata, Wild Himalayan Cherry bark, Vacha (sweet flag), Kali Musli

name: Malnad Kaavu
biodiversityInfo: Classic Malnad-style village grove with 130 plant species. Giant fig trees over 200 years old. Fruit bats colony of 2000+. Rare Malabar gliding frog.
scientificInfo: Old-growth fig-dominant forest. Keystone fig trees support 87 vertebrate species through fruiting cycles. Critical dry-season food source for wildlife.
climateRole: Prevents soil erosion on laterite slopes. Fig tree canopy reduces peak summer temperatures by 5°C in surrounding homesteads.
waterRole: Ancient village tank (kere) beside grove maintained by community. Grove root systems prevent tank siltation.
legendStory: The Kshetrapalaka deity is the guardian of village boundaries. Legend says a young farmer who tried to clear this grove for cultivation fell into a trance for 7 days. Upon awakening, he vowed never to harm the grove again. The grove has since been the site of village conflict resolution ceremonies.
traditions: Village court sessions held under main banyan tree. Youth initiation ceremonies. Annual boundary walk with temple priest.
ritualPractices: Offering of first fruits from every harvest. Fire walking ceremony during Shivaratri. Community feast under grove canopy.
waterSources: Village tank maintained by grove root systems. Two seasonal springs inside grove.
ecologicalImportance: Fruit bat colony critical for seed dispersal across 15km radius. Provides forest edge habitat for raptors. Night roost for 400+ bird species during migration.
rareMedicinalPlants: Bhumyamalaki, Amalaki, Haritaki, Vibhitaki (three fruits of Triphala), Guduchi

name: Yellapur Bana
biodiversityInfo: Semi-evergreen Bana with high orchid diversity. 18 species of orchids recorded. Brown palm civet and small Indian civet inhabit dense undergrowth.
scientificInfo: High epiphyte biomass indicating pristine air quality. Serves as regional orchid gene bank. Associated with unique mycorrhizal community.
climateRole: Transpiration from dense canopy adds measurable humidity to local atmosphere. Reduces fire risk in surrounding plantations.
waterRole: Contributes to Kali river headwater discharge. Underground spring active year-round serving 200 families.
legendStory: Goddess Yellamma is said to have rested in this grove after her epic journey from the coast. The ground where she sat became eternally fertile. A devadasi tradition honoring her was conducted here for centuries, now celebrated as a cultural heritage festival.
traditions: Yellamma Jatre festival. Devadasi heritage performance. Community singing of folk songs at grove boundary.
ritualPractices: Offering of neem leaves and turmeric to Yellamma stone. Lighting of earthen lamps at dusk. Women's collective singing ritual.
waterSources: Perennial spring inside grove. Seasonal stream feeding into Kali river.
ecologicalImportance: Orchid diversity hotspot of district-level significance. Palm civet seed dispersal for fruit-bearing trees. Pollinator reserve for arecanut plantations.
rareMedicinalPlants: Orchid tubers (Salep), Pepper (long variety), Nutmeg, Wild cardamom seeds, Rauwolfia

name: Mangalore Tulu Kaavu
biodiversityInfo: Coastal kaavu with mangrove-adjacent ecosystem. 95 plant species including coastal endemics. Estuarine birds year-round. Horseshoe crabs nearby.
scientificInfo: Transitional coastal forest fragment. Connects inland forest with Netravathi estuary. Essential wintering ground for trans-Himalayan migratory birds.
climateRole: Coastal windbreak reducing cyclone impact on village. Salt-spray-tolerant trees create inland humidity gradient.
waterRole: Prevents saltwater intrusion into freshwater wells. Root network stabilizes coastal soil preventing erosion.
legendStory: In Tulu tradition, Bhutas (spirit beings) are believed to be forest guardians who were once humans. This Kaavu is home to Panjurli (boar spirit) and Guliga. Annual Bhuta Kola performance where a priest channels the Bhuta spirit has been conducted uninterrupted for 300 years.
traditions: Annual Bhuta Kola ritual drama. No hunting within 2km. Coconut offerings during harvest season.
ritualPractices: Bhuta Kola performance by designated community priest. Fire torch procession. Collective oath-taking at grove boundary stone.
nativeSpeciesJson: ["Indian Laurel","Coconut Palm","Screw Pine","Mangrove Species","Wild Pepper","Sacred Tulsi"]
birdSpeciesJson: ["Greater Flameback","Indian Cormorant","Lesser Adjutant Stork","Pacific Golden Plover"]
waterSources: Tidal creek interface. Freshwater spring preventing salt intrusion.
ecologicalImportance: Estuarine bird habitat. Fish nursery adjacent to Netravathi estuary. Horseshoe crab spawning protection. Carbon sequestration in coastal soil.
rareMedicinalPlants: Surpunka (coastal medicinal), Punarnava, Indian sarsaparilla, Coastal Brahmi variant

name: Chikkamagaluru Forest Devara Kaadu
biodiversityInfo: Coffee-adjacent sacred grove preserving original shola forest species. 240 plant species. Indian bison (gaur) known to visit. Leopard camera-trapped.
scientificInfo: Critical genetic reservoir for shola oak and wild coffee species. Soil biome unique to undisturbed coffee forest transition zones.
climateRole: Shade regulation for surrounding coffee estates reduces water requirement by 25%. Natural windbreak preventing frost damage.
waterRole: Headwaters of Bhadravathi tributary. Coffee estate irrigation tanks recharged by grove groundwater.
legendStory: Chamundeshwari, the warrior goddess, is believed to have slain a demon in these hills, and where her divine weapon fell, this grove grew. Coffee planters of Mudigere have always observed the tradition of not planting within 100 meters of grove boundary, attributing their good harvests to the goddess's blessing.
traditions: Pre-planting blessing ceremony for coffee estates. Forest walk during Navaratri. Community fire prevention patrol.
ritualPractices: Offering of coffee flowers to Chamundeshwari. Evening prayer at boundary stone. Collective bonfire at grove perimeter during Diwali.
birdSpeciesJson: ["Nilgiri Wood Pigeon","Malabar Parakeet","Indian Blue Robin","Tickell's Blue Flycatcher"]
waterSources: Bhadravathi tributary headwaters. 5 coffee estate tanks recharged by grove.
ecologicalImportance: Wild coffee genetic diversity preservation. Gaur salt lick within grove. Leopard territory providing top predator control for deer populations.
rareMedicinalPlants: Wild Coffee bark (tonic), Cardamom pods, Pepper root, Cinnamon bark, Wild vanilla
